Chapter 01 — NSCA 5th Edition

Structure &
Function
of Body Systems

Musculoskeletal · Neuromuscular · The foundation every CSCS question is built on.

Before We Start

Why Does This Chapter Matter?

To prescribe training intelligently, you need to understand the systems doing the work — from bone and joint to muscle fibre to the heart and lungs:

1
How the body is built to move
Skeleton, joints, connective tissue — the structural platform every rep is built on.
2
What happens inside muscle when it contracts
Fibre types, sarcomere mechanics, sliding filament theory, neuromuscular control, and proprioception.
3
How the cardiovascular & respiratory systems respond
Cardiac output, oxygen delivery, lung volumes — the engine and fuel system behind every training adaptation.
Chapter 01 — Roadmap

What We're Covering

Part A
The Skeleton
Bones, joints, connective tissue — the framework muscles pull on.
Part B
Muscle Structure
From whole muscle down to the sarcomere — macro to micro.
Part C
Contraction Mechanism
Sliding filament theory — how a nerve signal becomes movement.
Part D
Neuromuscular System
Motor units, fiber types, recruitment — the control system.
Part E
Proprioception
Muscle spindles and GTOs — the body's feedback sensors.
Part G
Cardiovascular System
Heart, blood vessels, key equations — oxygen delivery to working muscle.
Part H
Respiratory System
Lung volumes, ventilatory threshold — gas exchange and breathing mechanics.
A
Part A

The
Skeleton

206 bones. The scaffolding, the anchor points, and the lever arms — all in one system.

Part A — The Skeleton

Bone is Alive

Not passive scaffolding. Living tissue that adapts to every load you place on it.

Wolff's Law
Bone grows and strengthens where it is stressed. Remove the stress — bone weakens. Apply progressive load — bone adapts. This is the biological basis of resistance training.
S&C Implication
High-impact and high-load training builds bone density. Gymnastics, weightlifting, and sprinting athletes have the highest bone mineral density of any sport group.
Bone Cells
Osteoblasts build bone. Osteoclasts break it down. Training tips the balance toward building — if load is appropriate and recovery is adequate.
Source: Labster Theory
Part A — The Skeleton
Axial vs Appendicular
AXIAL — The Core
Skull + Vertebral Column + Ribs + Sternum.
Vertebral column: 7 cervical · 12 thoracic · 5 lumbar · 5 sacral · 3–5 coccygeal
APPENDICULAR — The Limbs
Shoulder girdle + Arms + Pelvic girdle + Legs. All the parts that "append" to the central axis.
Memory aid: Axial = the AXIS (central core). Appendicular = the APPENDAGES (arms and legs) that hang off it.
Source: teachpe.com
Part A — The Skeleton
The Three Joint Types

Junctions where bones meet. The type determines how much movement is allowed.

Type 01
Fibrous Joints
Virtually no movement. Example: sutures of the skull. Bones connected by fibrous connective tissue.
Type 02
Cartilaginous Joints
Limited movement. Example: intervertebral discs. Bones connected by cartilage — allows slight flex and cushioning.
Type 03
Synovial Joints
Considerable movement. Example: elbow, knee, hip. Enclosed in a capsule filled with synovial fluid. Articulating surfaces covered with hyaline cartilage.

Sport and exercise movements mainly occur at synovial joints. Their key features: low friction and large range of motion.

Source: internet
Part A — Synovial Joints
Joint Movement Axes

Joints are classified by how many axes (directions) they can rotate about.

Uniaxial
1 Plane
Rotates about only ONE axis.
Example: Elbow (hinge joint). Flexion and extension only.

Note: The knee looks uniaxial but its axis of rotation actually changes throughout ROM.
Biaxial
2 Planes
Movement about TWO perpendicular axes.
Example: Ankle, wrist. Flexion/extension + lateral movement.
Multiaxial
3 Planes
Movement in ALL three planes.
Example: Hip and shoulder (ball-and-socket). Maximum range — maximum injury risk trade-off.
Stability vs Mobility trade-off: More axes = more range = less inherent stability. The shoulder can do almost anything — and dislocates more than any other joint.
Source: knyamed.com
Part A — Connective Tissues
Tendons, Ligaments & Cartilage
Tendon
Muscle → Bone
Connects muscle to bone periosteum. The epimysium (outer muscle wrapping) is contiguous with the tendon — so tension from a contracting muscle fiber is transmitted all the way to the bone.
Ligament
Bone → Bone
Connects bone to bone across joints. Stabilises the joint. Does not actively contract. Injury = sprain (Grade I–III).
Hyaline Cartilage
Joint Surface
Smooth cartilage covering articulating bone ends inside synovial joints. Reduces friction. No blood supply — heals poorly when damaged.
The chain: Muscle fiber → Endomysium → Perimysium → Epimysium → Tendon → Bone periosteum → Bone. Every layer is contiguous.
Part A — Summary

The Skeleton — Key Takeaways

Bone is living tissue
Remodels via osteoblasts/osteoclasts; responds to mechanical load
206 bones, 3 types
Long, short, flat — each suited to function
3 synovial joint types
Uniaxial (hinge), Biaxial (condyloid), Multiaxial (ball-&-socket)
Connective tissue matters
Tendons (bone→muscle), Ligaments (bone→bone) — transmit & stabilise force
Wolff's Law
Bone adapts its structure to the loads placed upon it
B
Part B

Muscle
Structure

From whole muscle to the smallest contractile unit — zoom in, layer by layer.

Source: lecturio.com
Part B — Muscle Structure
The Zoom-In Framework

Each level wraps the one inside it. Force transmits from the tiniest protein to bone.

Whole Muscle
Covered by epimysium (deep fascia). Connects to bone via tendon. Contains 430+ skeletal muscles in the body.
Fasciculus
Bundle of up to 150 muscle fibers, wrapped in perimysium. Visible as the grain in a steak.
Muscle Fiber
Single muscle cell. 50–100µm diameter (≈ a hair). Wrapped in endomysium + sarcolemma (cell membrane). Interior = sarcoplasm.
Myofibril
Hundreds per fiber. ~1µm diameter (1/100 of a hair). The contractile machinery of the muscle fiber. Contains myofilaments.
Myofilaments
Myosin (thick, ~16nm) and Actin (thin, ~6nm). Organized into the sarcomere — the smallest contractile unit.
Myofibril — thin and thick filaments, Z-lines and sarcomeres
Part B — Muscle Structure
Inside the Myofibril
Z-LINE
The boundary of each sarcomere. Actin filaments anchor here.
M-LINE
Centre of sarcomere. Thick myosin filaments anchor here.
THICK
Myosin filament (~16nm). Contains globular heads that form crossbridges.
THIN
Actin filament (~6nm). Two strands in a double helix. Has binding sites for myosin.
6 actin filaments surround each myosin filament — a lattice-like arrangement for maximum crossbridge formation efficiency.
Sarcomere: I-band, A-band, H-zone, M-line, Z-line
Part B — The Sarcomere
Reading the Bands
A-BAND
Dark band. Full width of myosin filament. Contains both actin and myosin overlap zone. Does NOT change during contraction.
I-BAND
Light band. Actin only — no myosin overlap. Exists in adjacent sarcomeres. SHRINKS during contraction as actin slides inward.
H-ZONE
Centre of A-band. Myosin only — no actin overlap at rest. SHRINKS during contraction as actin invades it.
Z-LINE
Sarcomere boundary. Z-lines are pulled CLOSER together as sarcomere shortens.
Exam trap: The A-band stays the same. Students often say it shrinks. It does NOT — myosin length is fixed. Only I-band and H-zone shrink.
Part B — The Sarcomere

The Gate Keepers

Actin alone isn't enough. Two regulatory proteins control whether contraction can happen at all.

Troponin — The Lock
Binding trigger
A protein that sits at regular intervals along the actin filament. Has a high affinity for calcium. When Ca²⁺ binds troponin, it changes shape — initiating a shift in tropomyosin.
Tropomyosin — The Gate
Binding blocker
A protein that runs along the length of the actin filament, covering the myosin binding sites at rest. When troponin shifts, tropomyosin moves — exposing the binding sites so myosin can attach.
Calcium — The Key
The trigger
Stored in the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR). Released when an action potential arrives via the T-tubule system. Binds troponin → gates open → contraction begins.
The analogy: Tropomyosin is a bouncer blocking the door. Calcium is the VIP pass. When Ca²⁺ arrives, troponin changes shape, tropomyosin steps aside, myosin walks in.
Part B — Summary

Muscle Structure — Key Takeaways

Hierarchical organisation
Muscle → Fascicle → Fibre → Myofibril → Sarcomere
Sarcomere = functional unit
Z-line to Z-line; contains actin (thin) & myosin (thick) filaments
Troponin & Tropomyosin
Gate the actin binding site — Ca²⁺ is the key that unlocks it
Connective tissue wraps
Epimysium → Perimysium → Endomysium — transmit tension to tendon
Myosin has ATPase activity
Energy for crossbridge cycling comes directly from ATP hydrolysis
C
Part C

Sliding
Filament
Theory

Proposed by Huxley & Hanson in 1954. The molecular explanation of how a nerve signal becomes movement.

Part C — Sliding Filament Theory

The Big Question

"A motor neuron fires.
A muscle shortens.
What actually happens in between?"
6
Key players involved
5
Phases of the process
5
Steps in the crossbridge cycle
1
Rule that determines force

The answer is called the Sliding Filament Theory. Actin slides over myosin. That's it at the highest level. The rest is understanding how and why.

Source: edurev.in
Part C — Sliding Filament Theory

Meet the Players

Before the mechanism — know who does what. Every player has one job.

The Track
Actin (Thin)
The thin filament. Has binding sites for myosin — but they're blocked at rest. Anchored at Z-lines.
The Engine
Myosin (Thick)
The thick filament. Has globular heads that reach out and grab actin. Contains ATPase enzyme. Anchored at M-line.
The Lock
Troponin
Sits on actin at regular intervals. Has high affinity for Ca²⁺. When bound by calcium, changes shape — triggers tropomyosin to move.
The Gate
Tropomyosin
Runs along actin, blocking binding sites at rest. When troponin shifts — it moves aside. Gates open. Myosin can now bind.
The Key
Calcium (Ca²⁺)
Stored in sarcoplasmic reticulum. Released when an action potential arrives. Binds troponin → unlocks everything.
The Fuel
ATP
Powers the myosin head. Without ATP: heads can't cock, can't detach. No ATP = no contraction AND no relaxation.
Source: ecampusontario.pressbooks.pub
Part C — Sliding Filament Theory

The 5 Phases

Phase 1
Resting
Low Ca²⁺ in myofibrils. Most stored in SR. Tropomyosin blocks binding sites. Minimal crossbridges.
Phase 2
EC Coupling
Action potential → T-tubules → SR releases Ca²⁺ → Ca²⁺ binds troponin → tropomyosin shifts → binding sites EXPOSED.
Phase 3
Contraction
Myosin heads bind actin. Power stroke pulls actin toward M-line. ATP hydrolyzed → ADP + Pᵢ.
Phase 4
Recharge
Cycle continues as long as Ca²⁺ present, ATP available for detachment, and ATPase active to break ATP down.
Phase 5
Relaxation
Motor nerve stops. Ca²⁺ pumped back to SR (needs ATP). Tropomyosin covers sites. Crossbridges detach.
Key insight: Relaxation is ACTIVE — it requires ATP to pump calcium back. This is why fatigue affects both force production AND the ability to relax fully.
Source: biorender.com
Part C — The Crossbridge Cycle
5 Steps of the Crossbridge Cycle
Step 1 — Energise
ATP Hydrolysis
Myosin ATPase enzyme hydrolyzes ATP → ADP + Pᵢ. Both remain bound to the myosin head. The energy released cocks the myosin head into a high-energy (armed) state. Ready to fire.
Step 2 — Bind
Crossbridge Formation
The energised myosin head binds to the exposed actin binding site — forming the actin-myosin crossbridge. This is only possible because Ca²⁺ already moved tropomyosin out of the way.
Step 3 — Pull
The Power Stroke
Pᵢ is released from the myosin head — this triggers a conformational change. The myosin head pivots (power stroke) — pulling the actin filament toward the centre of the sarcomere. ADP is then released.
Step 4 — Release
Detachment
A new ATP molecule binds to the myosin head. This causes the actin-myosin crossbridge to detach. The myosin head returns to its original low-energy state and shape.
Step 5 — Reset
Cycle Repeats
The new ATP is hydrolyzed again → myosin head re-cocks → ready to bind again. The cycle repeats as long as Ca²⁺, ATP and ATPase are all present.
Source: microbenotes.com
Part C — Sarcomere Mechanics
What Changes During Contraction
A-BAND
UNCHANGED
Myosin filament length is fixed. The A-band is the full width of the myosin — it never changes.
I-BAND
SHRINKS
Actin slides inward — the zone of bare actin gets smaller as it enters the A-band region.
H-ZONE
SHRINKS
Actin invades the H-zone — the bare myosin zone gets smaller as actin slides in from both sides.
Z-LINES
GET CLOSER
Pulled toward the centre as the sarcomere shortens. Distance between Z-lines = sarcomere length.
Length-Tension Relationship
Stretched muscle — I-bands and H-zone extended. Low crossbridge-actin alignment → LOW force potential.
Optimal length — Maximum actin-myosin overlap → MAXIMUM crossbridge formation → PEAK force.
Fully contracted — Actin overlaps past myosin → suboptimal alignment → force declines again.
Part C — Key Principle

The One Force Rule

The Rule
"The number of actin-myosin crossbridges formed within the muscle fibers of a given muscle at any instant in time dictates the force production of the muscle."
More Motor Units
More muscle fibers activated → more crossbridges simultaneously → more force. This is recruitment.
Faster Firing
Same motor units fire more rapidly → summation → crossbridges overlap in time → more force. This is rate coding.
Bigger Fibers
More myofibrils per fiber → more crossbridges per fiber → more force. This is hypertrophy.

Every training strategy — heavy loads, speed training, hypertrophy work — ultimately works by increasing the number of crossbridges formed at once.

Part C — Summary

Sliding Filament — Key Takeaways

Ca²⁺ is the trigger
Released from SR via T-tubule signal; binds troponin to expose actin
5-phase crossbridge cycle
Attach → Power stroke → Detach → Re-cock → Re-attach
Filaments slide, not shorten
A-band constant; I-band & H-zone narrow as sarcomere shortens
ATP required twice
For myosin energisation AND detachment from actin
Length-tension relationship
Optimal overlap = maximal force; extremes = less force
Force-velocity relationship
Slower contraction = greater force; faster = less
D
Part D

The Neuro-
muscular
System

How the nervous system controls muscle — from brain signal to crossbridge formation.

Source: docplexus.com
Part D — Neuromuscular System
The Motor Unit

One motor neuron + all the muscle fibers it innervates. When the neuron fires — ALL fibers contract simultaneously.

Definition
1 Motor Neuron + All its Muscle Fibers
A motor neuron and every muscle fiber it innervates form a single motor unit. When the neuron fires — ALL fibers in that unit contract simultaneously. There is no partial activation within a motor unit.
Size Varies by Precision Needed
Eye muscles: 1 fiber per motor neuron → ultra-precise control.
Quadriceps: Hundreds of fibers per neuron → large force, less precision.
The Neuromuscular Junction
Where axon terminal meets muscle fiber (motor end plate). Each muscle fiber has only one neuromuscular junction.
Part D — Neuromuscular System

The Neuromuscular Junction in Action

Watch how a nerve impulse crosses the synaptic cleft and triggers muscle contraction.

Click to watch on YouTube
YOUTUBE
The Neuromuscular Junction
Video: Neuromuscular Junction  ·  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nTZnBdeIb5c  ·  All rights reserved to the respective creator.
Source: ecampusontario.pressbooks.pub
Part D — Neuromuscular System

From Brain to Crossbridge

The complete signal chain — from motor cortex to myosin head in 6 steps.

1
Action Potential
Motor neuron fires — electrical impulse travels down axon to nerve terminal.
2
ACh Released
Acetylcholine secreted into the synaptic cleft at the neuromuscular junction.
3
Sarcolemma AP
ACh binds sarcolemma receptors → action potential generated along muscle fiber membrane.
4
T-Tubules
AP travels down T-tubules deep into the fiber — reaches the sarcoplasmic reticulum.
5
Ca²⁺ Released
SR releases calcium ions throughout the muscle fiber simultaneously.
6
Crossbridges
Ca²⁺ binds troponin → tropomyosin shifts → myosin binds actin → contraction.
All-or-None Principle: When a motor neuron fires, ALL fibers in its motor unit contract. No partial activation. The motor unit is the smallest controllable unit of force.
Source: pharmacy180.com
Part D — Force Grading
Twitch to Tetanus

A single nerve impulse produces a twitch. Repeated impulses produce sustained force. Here's how.

A
Single Twitch
One action potential → brief Ca²⁺ release → muscle contracts then relaxes. Lasts milliseconds. Low force. Calcium sequestered back before full force can develop.
B
Summation
Second AP arrives before first twitch ends → forces add together → greater force than a single twitch. Muscle hasn't fully relaxed before the second contraction.
C+D
Tetanus
High frequency → twitches fuse completely → smooth, sustained maximal force. This is the maximum force a motor unit can produce.

The ability of motor units to sustain fused tetanic contractions — along with motor unit size and recruitment — is a key determinant of maximal force in sport.

Part D — Summary

Neuromuscular System — Key Takeaways

Motor neuron → action potential
Signal travels along axon to NMJ via myelinated sheath
ACh crosses synaptic cleft
Binds nicotinic receptors → depolarises sarcolemma
Signal enters T-tubules
AP reaches SR → Ca²⁺ released into cytoplasm
All-or-None Law
A motor unit fires fully or not at all
Wave summation → Tetanus
Rapid re-stimulation before relaxation builds sustained force
Motor unit = neuron + fibres
One neuron controls many fibres; all of same fiber type
E
Part E

Muscle
Fiber
Types

Not all muscle fibers are equal. Type determines speed, force, and fatigue resistance.

Source: hiitscience.com
Part E — Muscle Fiber Types
Three Types, Three Profiles
Type I
Slow Oxidative
Small motor neuron. Low recruitment threshold — recruited first. Slow contraction and relaxation speed. High fatigue resistance. Low force output. High mitochondrial density. Red colour (myoglobin-rich). Ideal for: marathon, posture, endurance.
Type IIa
Fast Oxidative-Glycolytic
Large motor neuron. Intermediate-high threshold. Fast contraction. Intermediate fatigue resistance. High force output. Moderate mitochondria. White/red colour. The most adaptable fiber type — shifts characteristics with training.
Type IIx
Fast Glycolytic
Large motor neuron. High recruitment threshold — last recruited. Fastest contraction. Low fatigue resistance. Highest force and power output. Low mitochondria. White colour. Ideal for: sprinting, jumping, maximal lifts.
Key distinction: Type I myosin ATPase has a lower reaction rate → slower crossbridge cycling → slower but more sustainable. Type IIx ATPase is fast → rapid cycling → high force but rapid fatigue.
Part E — Muscle Fiber Types

Table 1.1 — Key Characteristics

Characteristic Type I Type IIa Type IIx
Motor neuron sizeSmallLargeLarge
Recruitment thresholdLowIntermediate/HighHigh
Contraction speedSlowFastFast
Fatigue resistanceHighIntermediate/LowLow
Force productionLowIntermediateHigh
Aerobic enzyme contentHighIntermediate/LowLow
Anaerobic enzyme contentLowHighHigh
Mitochondrial densityHighIntermediateLow
Capillary densityHighIntermediateLow
Myoglobin contentHighLowLow
Fiber diameterSmallIntermediateLarge
ColorRedWhite/RedWhite
Part E — Sport Applications

Fiber Types in Sport Events

Table 1.2 — The physiological demand of each sport determines which fiber types dominate.

Type II Dominant (High)
100m sprint · Olympic weightlifting · Basketball · Volleyball · Baseball pitcher · 50m swim · Field events (throws/jumps) · Bobsled · American football
Both Type I & II High
800m run · Soccer · MMA · Wrestling · Tennis · Downhill skiing · Speed skating · Rowing
Type I Dominant (High)
Marathon · Cross-country skiing · Distance cycling · Biathlon
The Rule
The higher the physiological demand per unit time, the more Type II recruitment required. Sprint end of a distance race? Type II activates. Near-maximal lift? ~90% of motor units activate.
Part E — Motor Unit Recruitment

How Force is Graded

Two mechanisms. Both increase the number of crossbridges formed at any instant.

Mechanism 1 — Rate Coding
Fire Faster
Increase the frequency of action potentials. Twitches begin to summate. As frequency rises → unfused → fused tetanus. More force from the SAME motor units. Dominant in small muscles (hand).
Mechanism 2 — Recruitment
Activate More
Increase the number of motor units active. Each new unit adds its fibers' crossbridges to the total. Dominant in large muscles (thigh). At maximal effort, ~90% of available motor units recruited.
The Order — Size Principle
Small First
Type I (small, low threshold) recruited first. Type IIa next. Type IIx (large, high threshold) last. Reversed only in certain explosive voluntary contractions.

Light load arm curl = mostly Type I. Heavy barbell curl = Type I + IIa + IIx all firing. Load determines which fibers train. This is the biological basis of training specificity.

Source: researchgate.net
Part E — Summary

Fiber Types & Recruitment — Key Takeaways

Type I — Slow Oxidative
High fatigue resistance, low force; dominant in endurance
Type IIx — Fast Glycolytic
High force & speed, rapid fatigue; sprint & max strength
Type IIa — Intermediate
Trainable toward either end; recruited in sustained high-intensity work
Size Principle (Henneman)
Type I → IIa → IIx recruited as force demand increases
Training shifts fiber type
Endurance → more IIa; heavy resistance → IIx → IIa conversion
F
Part F

Proprio-
ception

The body's feedback system — sensing position, length, and force without looking.

Part F — Proprioception

The Body's Feedback System

Proprioceptors are specialized sensory receptors in joints, muscles, and tendons. They relay information to the CNS — mostly subconsciously.

What they detect
Pressure & Tension
Proprioceptors are sensitive to mechanical changes — pressure, stretch, and tension. They provide the brain with kinesthetic sense: conscious appreciation of body position relative to gravity.
How it works
Mostly Subconscious
Most proprioceptive processing happens below conscious level — this is how we maintain posture and balance without thinking. Only a fraction reaches conscious awareness.
Two Key Receptors
Spindle + GTO
Two receptors do most of the work: Muscle Spindles (length sensors) and Golgi Tendon Organs (force sensors). They work in opposition — one facilitates, one inhibits.

CSCS exam definition: "Proprioceptors are specialized sensory receptors that provide the CNS with information needed to maintain muscle tone and perform complex coordinated movements."

Part F — Proprioception
Two Sensors, Two Jobs
MUSCLE SPINDLE
Located inside the muscle, parallel to extrafusal fibers (intrafusal fibers). Detects muscle LENGTH + rate of change. Stretch → spindle fires → reflex contraction. Accelerator.
GOLGI TENDON ORGAN (GTO)
Located at the myotendinous junction, in series with muscle fibers. Detects FORCE/TENSION. High force → GTO fires → inhibitory interneuron → muscle relaxes. The brake.
Memory aid: Spindle = Stretch sensor = Stimulates. GTO = force/Tension sensor = Turns off (inhibits).
Part F — Proprioception
Two Reflexes
STRETCH REFLEX (via Spindle)
Muscle stretched → spindle fires → sensory neuron → spinal cord synapses with motor neuron → muscle contracts. Knee jerk reflex is the classic example. In training: the stretch-shortening cycle exploits this for power.
AUTOGENIC INHIBITION (via GTO)
Excessive tension → GTO fires → sensory neuron → spinal cord synapses with inhibitory interneuron → motor neuron inhibited → muscle relaxes. Protective against tendon rupture.
S&C APPLICATION
Heavy resistance training may progressively reduce GTO inhibition — the motor cortex overrides the inhibitory signal. This neural adaptation is one reason strength increases before hypertrophy.
Part F — Summary

Spindle vs GTO — Side by Side

Muscle Spindle
Golgi Tendon Organ
Inside the muscle belly
At the myotendinous junction
Parallel to muscle fibers
In series with muscle fibers
Detects: MUSCLE LENGTH + rate of stretch
Detects: FORCE / TENSION
Response: Facilitates contraction (stretch reflex)
Response: Inhibits contraction (autogenic inhibition)
Example: Knee jerk reflex, plyometric rebound
Example: Extreme load causes "buckling" — protective

Spindles and GTOs work in opposition. Together they regulate muscle activation to optimise force and prevent injury.

Part F — Summary

Proprioception — Key Takeaways

Muscle Spindle
Detects LENGTH & rate of stretch → triggers stretch reflex
Golgi Tendon Organ (GTO)
Detects TENSION → autogenic inhibition (protective relaxation)
Stretch Reflex
Rapid, monosynaptic; spindle → spinal cord → agonist contraction
Autogenic Inhibition
GTO → inhibitory interneuron → agonist relaxes to protect tissue
Reciprocal Inhibition
Agonist firing automatically inhibits antagonist via Ia afferents
G
Part G

The
Cardio-
vascular
System

Heart anatomy, valves, cardiac conduction, key formulas, blood vessels, and acute and chronic training responses.

Source: easy-peasy.ai
Part G — The Cardiovascular System
Four Chambers, Four Valves

The heart is two pumps in one. Right side = pulmonary (low-pressure) circuit. Left side = systemic (high-pressure) circuit.

Right Side → Lungs
Pulmonary Circuit
SVC/IVC → RA → tricuspid valve → RV → pulmonary valve → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary veins → LA
Left Side → Body
Systemic Circuit
LA → mitral valve → LV → aortic valve → aorta → body → SVC/IVC → RA
AV valves (tricuspid, mitral) prevent backflow into atria during systole. Semilunar valves (pulmonary, aortic) prevent backflow from arteries into ventricles during diastole.
LV wall is ~3× thicker than RV — it generates the higher pressure required to drive blood through the entire systemic circulation.
Source: vetscraft.com
Part G — The Cardiovascular System
Cardiac Conduction: SA → AV → Purkinje
SA Node — the pacemaker. Located in the upper lateral wall of the right atrium. Intrinsic discharge rate: 60–80 times per minute. Because this rate is faster than the AV node (40–60/min) or ventricular fibres (15–40/min), the SA node sets the rhythm for the whole heart.
AV Node — the gatekeeper. Located in the posterior septal wall of the right atrium. Delays the impulse slightly, allowing the atria to empty blood into the ventricles before ventricular contraction begins.
AV Bundle (Bundle of His) → Left & Right Bundle Branches. Rapid conduction pathway down the interventricular septum. These fibres have characteristics opposite to AV nodal fibres — large, fast-conducting — so the signal travels quickly toward the apex.
Purkinje Fibres — the spark plugs. Spread the impulse rapidly throughout the ventricular walls, causing both ventricles to contract at approximately the same time from apex upward, efficiently ejecting blood.
Sequence Mnemonic
SA
→
AV
→
HIS
→
PURKINJE
"Some Athletes Have Pure Dedication"
Part G — The Cardiovascular System

The ECG: P · QRS · T

Each wave on the ECG reflects a specific electrical event in the heart. The P and QRS record depolarisation; the T records repolarisation.

First Wave
P-Wave
Atrial depolarisation. Small rounded wave caused by changes in electrical potential that depolarise the atria, resulting in atrial contraction.
Second Complex
QRS Complex
Ventricular depolarisation. Generated by the electrical potential that depolarises the ventricles, causing ventricular contraction. Also masks atrial repolarisation, which occurs simultaneously but is hidden by the larger QRS signal.
Third Wave
T-Wave
Ventricular repolarisation. Caused by the electrical potential generated as ventricles recover from depolarisation. This process (repolarisation) occurs in ventricular muscle shortly after depolarisation.
Source: a-fib.com

P = atria depolarise & contract. QRS = ventricles depolarise & contract (atrial repolarisation hidden here). T = ventricles repolarise. One full cycle = one heartbeat.

Part G — The Cardiovascular System

The Cardiovascular System Explained

A visual walkthrough of cardiac output, blood flow and oxygen delivery — the full CV picture.

YOUTUBE
The Cardiovascular System

Part G — The Cardiovascular System

The Key Formulas

Five named equations from this chapter. Know each symbol, its units, and a worked example.

Cardiac Output (Eq. 1.1)
Q̇ = SV × HR
Q̇
Cardiac Output (L·min⁻¹)
SV
Stroke Volume (mL·beat⁻¹)
HR
Heart Rate (beats·min⁻¹)
Rest: ~5 L·min⁻¹
Max exercise: ~20–22 L·min⁻¹
(untrained; higher in trained)
Fick Equation (Eq. 1.2)
V̇O₂ = Q̇ × a-v̄O₂ diff
V̇O₂
O₂ consumption (mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹)
a-v̄O₂
Arteriovenous O₂ difference
Example: 72 bpm × 65 mL/beat
× 6 mL O₂/100 mL = 281 mL O₂/min
÷ 80 kg = 3.5 mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹
MAP (Eq. 1.4)
DBP + ⅓(SBP − DBP)
HRmax
220 − Age
Rate–Pressure Product (Eq. 1.3)
HR × SBP

Rate–pressure product estimates myocardial O₂ consumption. Fick is the master equation: V̇O₂ = HR × SV × a-vO₂ difference — all three can be trained.

Part G — Key Equations · 1 of 5

Q̇ = SV × HR — Cardiac Output

Cardiac output is how much blood your heart pumps every minute. It is the single most important measure of cardiovascular performance.

What is Q̇?
Cardiac Output
Think of it as the heart's throughput. If your heart is a pump, Q̇ is how many litres of blood it delivers to the body per minute. At rest you need ~5 L/min. Sprinting, you may need 20+ L/min. Your body's ability to meet that demand determines your aerobic ceiling.
What is SV?
Stroke Volume
Volume of blood ejected per beat. Like how much water one pump stroke pushes out. Resting SV untrained: ~65–70 mL. Trained endurance athletes: up to 160 mL/beat. Increases with greater venous return (Frank-Starling) and sympathetic stimulation.
What is HR?
Heart Rate
How many times the heart beats per minute. Resting HR: 60–100 bpm. Trained athletes: as low as 28–40 bpm (bradycardia). HRmax ≈ 220 − age. HR rises linearly with exercise intensity.
Worked Example — Same resting Q̇, very different physiology
SEDENTARY PERSON
70 mL × 72 bpm = 5.0 L/min
ENDURANCE ATHLETE (same resting Q̇)
100 mL × 50 bpm = 5.0 L/min

Same resting Q̇ — but the athlete achieves it with larger stroke volume + fewer beats per minute, leaving far more cardiac reserve for exercise.

Part G — Key Equations · 2 of 5

Fick: V̇O₂ = Q̇ × a-v̄O₂ diff

The Fick equation tells you how much oxygen the body is actually consuming. It links cardiac output (delivery) to how much O₂ the muscles actually extract.

What is V̇O₂?
Oxygen Consumption
How much O₂ your body uses per minute per kg bodyweight. At rest: ~3.5 mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹ (= 1 MET). During maximal exercise untrained: 25–45 mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹. Elite endurance athletes: 70–85 mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹. The higher your V̇O₂max, the higher your aerobic ceiling.
What is a-v̄O₂ diff?
Arteriovenous O₂ Difference
How much O₂ the tissues extract from the blood. Arterial blood arrives with ~20 mL O₂/100 mL. Venous blood returns with ~15 mL O₂/100 mL. Difference = 5 mL/100 mL at rest → 15+ mL/100 mL during maximal exercise as muscles work harder.
The two levers
Delivery × Extraction
V̇O₂max is limited by BOTH sides. Central: how much blood the heart delivers (Q̇max). Peripheral: how much O₂ the muscles extract (a-v̄O₂ diff). Training improves both — larger heart for delivery, more mitochondria and capillaries for extraction.
Worked Example — Textbook Eq. 1.2 (80 kg athlete at rest)
HR 72 bpm × SV 65 mL/beat × a-v̄O₂ diff 6 mL O₂/100 mL = 281 mL O₂/min
281 ÷ 80 kg = 3.5 mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹ = 1 MET — resting metabolic rate

V̇O₂max = Q̇max × max a-v̄O₂ diff. Train the heart for delivery. Train the muscles for extraction. Both sides of Fick drive V̇O₂max higher.

Part G — Key Equations · 3 of 5

MAP = DBP + ⅓(SBP − DBP)

Mean Arterial Pressure is the average blood pressure across one full cardiac cycle — a better indicator of tissue perfusion than systolic or diastolic alone.

What is SBP?
Systolic Blood Pressure
Peak pressure when ventricles contract and eject blood. Normal resting: 110–139 mmHg. Maximal aerobic exercise: 220–260 mmHg. Heavy resistance exercise: up to 320 mmHg has been recorded. Measured by the upper number on a BP cuff reading.
What is DBP?
Diastolic Blood Pressure
Pressure when ventricles relax and fill. Normal resting: 60–89 mmHg. During aerobic exercise, DBP stays near resting or drops slightly due to vasodilation in active muscles. This drop in DBP with aerobic exercise is clinically significant.
Why ⅓ and not ½?
Diastole Takes Longer
The heart spends approximately ⅔ of each cardiac cycle in diastole (filling) and only ⅓ in systole (contracting). So the average pressure across the whole cycle is weighted toward diastole — MAP is always less than (SBP + DBP) ÷ 2.
Worked Example — Resting BP 120/80 mmHg
MAP = 80 + ⅓ × (120 − 80) = 80 + ⅓ × 40 = 80 + 13.3 = 93.3 mmHg Simple average would be 100 mmHg — MAP is always lower

Common mistake: using (SBP + DBP) ÷ 2. MAP = DBP + ⅓(pulse pressure). The heart rests longer than it contracts — always weight toward diastole.

Part G — Key Equations · 4 of 5

HRmax = 220 − Age

The most widely used formula to estimate maximal heart rate. Simple and practical — but understand its limitations and why it matters for training zone prescription.

Why HR declines with age
SA Node Ageing
The SA node loses pacemaker cells as we age. This structurally limits how fast the heart can fire. HRmax declines approximately 1 beat per year regardless of fitness level — even elite athletes follow this decline, though their absolute values remain higher.
Accuracy
±10–12 bpm SD
This formula has a standard deviation of ±10–12 bpm — so for a 47-year-old, estimated HRmax = 173 bpm, but actual could fall anywhere from 161–185 bpm. An alternative with better accuracy for healthy adults: 208 − 0.7 × age.
How it is used
Training Zone Anchor
HRmax anchors all heart-rate-based intensity zones. Zone 2 endurance ≈ 60–70% HRmax. Threshold ≈ 80–90% HRmax. VT in trained athletes occurs at ~70–90% HRmax. Use it as a starting point, then adjust based on RPE and real-world performance.
HRmax across the lifespan (220 − age)
AGE 20
200
bpm
AGE 30
190
bpm
AGE 40
180
bpm
AGE 50
170
bpm
AGE 65
155
bpm

HRmax declines ~1 bpm/year regardless of fitness. Use it as an estimate — adjust zones based on actual RPE and performance data.

Part G — Key Equations · 5 of 5

Rate–Pressure Product = HR × SBP

Also called the double product (Eq. 1.3). It estimates the oxygen demand placed on the heart muscle itself — how hard the myocardium is working, independent of the rest of the body.

What it measures
Myocardial O₂ Demand
The heart muscle needs its own O₂ supply via the coronary arteries. Higher HR = more contractions per minute. Higher SBP = greater force per contraction. Their product estimates myocardial oxygen consumption — the cardiac "cost" of any given workload.
Clinical use
Ischaemic Threshold
In patients with coronary artery disease, angina tends to occur at a reproducible RPP value. Exercise testing uses RPP to identify the workload at which myocardial O₂ demand exceeds supply — a key safety marker during graded exercise tests.
Training effect
↓ RPP at Same Workload
Endurance training reduces RPP at any given submaximal workload — lower resting HR and lower SBP mean the heart accomplishes the same external work with less internal effort. A trained heart is a more efficient heart.
Worked Example — RPP at rest vs maximal exercise
AT REST
72 × 120
8,640
Low cardiac demand
MODERATE EXERCISE
140 × 170
23,800
~2.8× resting demand
MAXIMAL AEROBIC
185 × 220
40,700
~5× resting demand

Equation 1.3 in the textbook. RPP tells you how hard the heart itself is working — not just how much blood it's delivering to the body.

Source: mydraw.com
Part G — The Cardiovascular System
Blood Vessels: Artery to Vein
Artery
Thick muscular walls. High pressure. Rapidly transport blood away from heart.
Arteriole
Control vessels. Smooth muscle can close completely or dilate many times their size — primary regulator of blood flow to capillaries.
Capillary
One cell thick, permeable. Exchange O₂, CO₂, nutrients, electrolytes, hormones with interstitial fluid.
Venule
Collect blood from capillaries. Thin wall, low pressure.
Vein
Large lumen, thin but muscular walls. One-way valves prevent backflow. Blood reservoir — 64% of total blood volume at rest.
Vasoconstriction (reduced vessel diameter) and vasodilation (increased vessel diameter) are the primary mechanisms for regulating regional blood flow. During vigorous exercise, up to 90% of cardiac output redirects to active muscle (vs 15–20% at rest).

Arterioles = the main resistance vessels. Vasodilation of arterioles supplying active muscle is the primary mechanism for increasing blood flow during exercise.

Part G — The Cardiovascular System

Blood Vessels Explained

Arteries, veins, and capillaries — their structure, function, and role in delivering oxygen to working muscle.

Click to watch on YouTube
YOUTUBE
Blood Vessels: Arteries, Veins & Capillaries
Video: Blood Vessels Explained: Arteries, Veins, and Capillaries  ·  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5EiDGEJPK70  ·  All rights reserved to the respective creator.
Part G — The Cardiovascular System

Acute CV Responses to Aerobic Exercise

A single bout of aerobic exercise immediately stresses the cardiovascular system. These are the acute (short-term) changes that occur during the session.

Cardiac Output
↑ Q̇ up to 4–5×
Rises from ~5 L/min at rest to ~20–22 L/min at maximal exercise. Initially increases rapidly, then reaches a plateau at steady state. Both SV and HR contribute.
Stroke Volume
↑ SV (Frank-Starling)
Venous return increases (venoconstriction + skeletal muscle pump + respiratory pump) → greater end-diastolic volume → myocardial fibres stretch → more forceful contraction. This is the Frank-Starling mechanism. SV plateaus at ~40–50% VO₂max.
Heart Rate
↑ HR (linear with intensity)
Sympathetic stimulation increases HR linearly with exercise intensity. Even anticipation of exercise causes an anticipatory HR rise. HRmax ≈ 220 − age.
Blood Pressure
↑ Systolic, ↓ Diastolic
SBP rises with intensity (up to 220–260 mmHg at max aerobic exercise). DBP remains at resting level or slightly decreases due to vasodilation in active muscles.
Blood Flow Distribution
↑ to Active Muscle
At rest ~15–20% of Q̇ goes to skeletal muscle. During vigorous exercise this rises to ~90% as arterioles supplying active muscle vasodilate and others constrict.
Anaerobic Exercise
Extreme BP Spikes
During resistance exercise, peak BP of 320/250 mmHg and HR of 170 bpm have been reported at 95% 1RM. BP response is higher during the concentric phase.

Frank-Starling mechanism: greater venous return → greater stretch → greater force of contraction → greater SV. This is why warm-up and venous return matter before maximal efforts.

ACUTE RESPONSE SNAPSHOT  ·  ↑ Q̇ (up to 4–5×)  ·  ↑ HR (linear with intensity)  ·  ↑ SV via Frank-Starling (plateaus at ~50% V̇O₂max)  ·  ↑ SBP  ·  ↓ DBP  ·  ↑ blood flow to active muscle (80–85% of Q̇)
Part G — The Cardiovascular System

Chronic Cardiac Adaptations to Training

Repeated aerobic exercise bouts remodel the heart structurally and functionally, permanently increasing its capacity to deliver oxygen.

Structural
↑ LV Chamber Volume
Eccentric hypertrophy — LV chamber volume enlarges. Greater end-diastolic volume = more blood fills the ventricle each beat. Wall thickness may also increase moderately.
Stroke Volume
↑ Maximal SV
Sedentary college-aged men: max SV ~100–120 mL. After endurance training: max SV rises to 150–160 mL in men, ~100–110 mL in women. Primary driver of higher Q̇max.
Heart Rate
↓ Resting HR
Higher vagal tone + increased SV means fewer beats maintain resting Q̇. Bradycardia (HR <60 bpm) is common in endurance-trained athletes. Elite athletes: 28–50 bpm.
Blood Volume
↑ Plasma Volume
Training increases total blood and plasma volume, improving venous return (preload) and cardiac filling — directly enhancing the Frank-Starling mechanism.
Resting Q̇
Unchanged (~5 L·min⁻¹)
Resting cardiac output stays the same — but it's achieved with fewer beats and a larger stroke volume after training. The efficiency improvement is the adaptation.
VO₂max
↑ via Q̇ + a-vO₂
Both central (↑ Q̇max via SV) and peripheral (↑ a-vO₂ diff via capillary density + mitochondria) adaptations increase VO₂max. Greatest training effect in untrained individuals.

Key number: trained men SV 150–160 mL vs sedentary 100–120 mL. That difference in SV, multiplied across every beat at maximal exercise, is what separates elite cardiac output from average.

Part G — Summary

Cardiovascular System — Key Takeaways

Q̇ = SV × HR
Rises from ~5 L/min rest to 20–22 L/min at max exercise
Fick Equation
V̇O₂ = Q̇ × a-vO₂ diff — links cardiac output to O₂ extraction
SV increases first
Frank-Starling dominates to ~40–60% V̇O₂max; then HR leads
Arteries → Capillaries → Veins
Capillaries are the O₂ & nutrient exchange site
Chronic adaptations
Cardiac hypertrophy, ↑ SV, ↓ resting HR, ↑ blood volume
Rate-Pressure Product
HR × SBP = myocardial O₂ demand; falls with training
Acute responses (single session)
↑ Q̇, ↑ HR (linear), ↑ SV (Frank-Starling to ~50% V̇O₂max), ↑ SBP, ↓ DBP, blood redistributed to active muscle
H
Part H

The
Respira-
tory
System

Airways, blood gas transport, lung volumes, ventilatory threshold, acute responses, and training adaptations.

Source: oer.hawaii.edu
Part H — The Respiratory System
From Nose to Alveolus

Air passes through ~23 generations of branching airways before reaching the alveoli. Trachea is the first-generation respiratory passage; left and right main bronchi are second-generation.

Conducting Zone
Nose → Bronchioles
Warms, humidifies, and purifies air. No gas exchange — purely a conduit. The non-functional volume here is the anatomical dead space (~150 mL in young adults).
Respiratory Zone
Alveoli
Site of gas exchange. Approximately 300 million alveoli with a total surface area of ~70 m². Walls are extremely thin, permeable to O₂ and CO₂.
Pathway Order
Nasal Cavity → Pharynx → Larynx → Trachea (1st gen) → Primary Bronchi (2nd gen) → Bronchioles (~23 gen total) → Alveolar Ducts → Alveoli
Source: studyclix.ie
Part H — The Respiratory System
Gas Exchange & Blood Transport

Diffusion follows partial pressure gradients. O₂ moves into blood; CO₂ moves out. Haemoglobin is the primary O₂ carrier.

Oxygen
O₂ → Blood → Muscle
Alveolar PO₂ = 105 mmHg > venous PO₂ = 40 mmHg → O₂ diffuses into capillary. At the tissue, arterial PO₂ = 100 mmHg > interstitial PO₂ ~40 mmHg → O₂ diffuses to muscle.
Carbon Dioxide
CO₂ → Blood → Exhaled
Venous PCO₂ = 46 mmHg > alveolar PCO₂ = 40 mmHg → CO₂ diffuses out and is exhaled. ~70% of CO₂ is transported as bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻).
Haemoglobin capacity: Men have ~15–16 g Hb per 100 mL blood. One gram of haemoglobin carries 1.34 mL O₂ → oxygen-carrying capacity ≈ 20 mL O₂ per 100 mL blood in men.
During high-intensity exercise: tissue PO₂ drops to ~3 mmHg and PCO₂ rises to ~90 mmHg — much steeper gradients than rest, dramatically accelerating gas exchange.
Part H — The Respiratory System

The Respiratory System Explained

How lungs, airways and gas exchange work together to supply oxygen and remove CO₂ during exercise.

Click to watch on YouTube
YOUTUBE
The Respiratory System
Video: The Respiratory System  ·  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=57byXpOUpSU  ·  All rights reserved to the respective creator.
Part H — The Respiratory System

Lung Volumes — Know Them All

Six volumes and capacities — and the anatomical dead space. The CSCS exam tests both names and the relationships between them.

TV
Tidal
~0.5 L
IRV
Inspiratory Reserve
~3.0 L
ERV
Expiratory Reserve
~1.2 L
RV
Residual (cannot exhale)
~1.2 L
VC
Vital Capacity = TV + IRV + ERV
~4.7 L
TLC
Total Lung Capacity = VC + RV
~5.9 L
Dead
~150 mL
Cannot Exhale
Residual Volume
Always remains in the lungs. Keeps alveoli open. Cannot be measured by spirometry — requires body plethysmography.
Dead Space
Anatomical Dead Space
~150 mL in young adults — the volume in conducting airways where no gas exchange occurs. Increases slightly as TV increases with deep breathing, but proportionally less than the TV increase.
During Max Exercise
TV → up to 3 L+
TV rises from ~0.4–1 L at rest to as much as 3 L or greater at maximal exercise. Breathing frequency rises from 12–15 to 35–45 breaths/min. Minute ventilation can reach 90–150 L/min.
Part H — The Respiratory System

Ventilatory Threshold (VT)

The intensity at which ventilation rises disproportionately to oxygen consumption — a key marker of aerobic fitness and training status.

What Causes It
Lactate Buffering → CO₂
As intensity rises, lactate accumulates and bicarbonate buffering produces excess CO₂. This CO₂ drives a non-linear increase in ventilation. At this point the ventilatory equivalent (VE/VO₂) increases from ~20–25 L/L to 35–40 L/L.
Untrained vs Trained
Occurs Earlier / Later
Untrained: VT ≈ 45–65% VO₂max. Trained endurance athletes: VT ≈ 70–90% VO₂max. Training shifts VT to a higher % of VO₂max — athletes can sustain higher intensities aerobically.
Field Significance
Lactate Threshold Proxy
VT closely parallels lactate threshold. Intensities just below VT can be sustained for long periods. Training zones (threshold, tempo) are anchored to VT or lactate threshold.
Source: frontiersin.org

VT = the aerobic ceiling before non-linear breathing kicks in. Trained athletes sustain higher absolute workloads below VT — that's the performance difference training creates.

Part H — The Respiratory System

Acute Responses & Chronic Adaptations

The respiratory system responds immediately to exercise and adapts with chronic training — though it is rarely the limiting factor in healthy individuals.

Acute — Aerobic Exercise
↑ Minute Ventilation
TV rises from 0.4–1 L to as much as 3 L+. Breathing frequency rises from 12–15 to 35–45 breaths/min. Minute ventilation (VE) can increase to 15–25× resting values (90–150 L/min) during strenuous exercise.
Acute — Anaerobic Exercise
VE Peaks Post-Set
During resistance exercise VE rises significantly each set, but peaks even higher in the first minute of recovery. Short rest intervals (30 s–1 min) produce the greatest ventilatory elevation. VE rates over 60 L/min have been reported.
Chronic Adaptation
↑ Efficiency
Trained individuals ventilate less at the same submaximal workload (lower VE). Higher TV rather than faster breathing rate is the more efficient strategy — dead space proportion stays small. VT shifts to higher % VO₂max.
Limiting Factor
Cardiac, Not Pulmonary
In healthy individuals, cardiac output — not respiratory capacity — limits VO₂max. Ventilation at rest uses only 3–5% of total energy; this may rise to 8–15% during heavy exercise.
Respiratory Muscles
↑ Fatigue Resistance
Both endurance and resistance training strengthen the diaphragm and intercostals. Resistance training particularly taxes these muscles via the Valsalva maneuver and intra-abdominal pressure demands.
Deeper Breathing
More Efficient Than Faster
Increasing tidal volume with exercise is proportionately greater than any increase in anatomical dead space — so deeper breathing improves alveolar ventilation more efficiently than increasing breathing rate alone.
Part H — Summary

Respiratory System — Key Takeaways

Lung volumes
TV ~0.5 L · IRV ~3 L · ERV ~1.2 L · RV ~1.2 L · VC ~4.7 L
RV cannot be exhaled
Keeps alveoli open; measured by body plethysmography only
TV rises with exercise
From ~0.5 L rest to ≥3 L at max; freq → 35–45 breaths/min
Ventilatory Threshold (VT)
Ventilation rises disproportionately — mirrors lactate threshold
Dead space ~150 mL
Conducting airways where no gas exchange occurs
Chronic adaptations
Stronger respiratory muscles, slight ↑ VC, more efficient exchange

Type I / IIa / IIx · Size Principle · Training shifts fiber type

Spindle (length) · GTO (tension) · Stretch reflex · Reciprocal inhibition
